scope_findings.claim: MAESTRO develops next-generation AI agent architectures powered by LLMs and/or multimodal models, capable of autonomous task execution through instruction interpretation, planning, tool use, and autonomous action to achieve high-level goals.

MAESTRO develops a hybrid neuro-symbolic planning engine that decomposes high-level goals into executable sub-task graphs with formally specified pre-conditions, post-conditions, and verification checkpoints, integrating Monte Carlo Tree Search and hierarchical task networks with LLM-driven heuristic evaluation to support robust planning and search over complex task horizons.

MAESTRO addresses self-correction through plan monitoring — detecting execution failures and replanning from verified intermediate states rather than restarting from scratch, enabling robust long-horizon agent behaviour.

MAESTRO designs an adaptive memory architecture integrating working memory (structured attention-managed short-term store for goals, active plan state, and recent observations), episodic memory (retrieval-augmented store of past task episodes indexed by context similarity), and semantic memory (persistent knowledge graph connecting domain concepts, tool capabilities, and learned task patterns), with a memory controller managing information flow between stores.

MAESTRO enhances AI agent autonomy through advanced self-planning, self-optimisation (neuro-symbolic planning engine, plan monitoring, self-correction) and innovation in memory-augmented AI agents for robust long-term reasoning and lifelong learning (episodic memory enabling learning from prior experience without retraining; semantic memory continuously updated through agent interactions).

MAESTRO develops advanced multi-agent frameworks for collaborative agents: decentralised coordination protocols enabling heterogeneous agents to collaborate via formal task-delegation protocols with provable convergence guarantees, conflict resolution mechanisms for contested resources and inconsistent world models, and auditable structured interaction traces for human inspection.

MAESTRO develops an External Tool and API Orchestration Layer — architecturally central — providing a typed tool registry with capability model, formal tool selection and invocation policies integrated with the neuro-symbolic planning engine, standardised API adapters (including REST, GraphQL, HL7 FHIR, OPC-UA), post-invocation verification and grounding, execution monitoring with fallback/recovery, and auditable tool execution traces; addressing the essential requirement for external tool and API integration to overcome limitations of large AI models and enhance performance in tasks requiring accuracy and reliability.

MAESTRO validates research in the healthcare Apply AI sector through a Clinical Decision Support demonstrator: an AI agent system assisting clinicians in diagnostic reasoning, integrating HL7 FHIR APIs to gather patient data from multiple sources, coordinating specialist sub-agents for imaging analysis, lab result interpretation, and treatment guideline retrieval, with full provenance tracking for regulatory auditability; validated with clinical partners against real diagnostic pathways.

MAESTRO validates research in the advanced manufacturing Apply AI sector through a Process Optimisation demonstrator: a multi-agent system autonomously monitoring, diagnosing, and optimising manufacturing processes in real time, with agents coordinating across production line segments using OPC-UA connectors, digital twin APIs, and sensor data streams; validated in operational factory environments with manufacturing partners.

MAESTRO incorporates appropriate validation and monitoring techniques for agent behaviour across all demonstrators: plan monitoring with self-correction, performance assessment of tool invocation success, reasoning trace completeness, multi-agent coordination latency, and diagnostic accuracy — integrated as required design elements.

MAESTRO incorporates qualitative and quantitative KPIs, standardised benchmarking across all demonstrators against operational baselines and current state-of-the-art agent systems, and structured progress monitoring at 6-month integration checkpoints.
MAESTRO allocates dedicated tasks for cohesion activities with ADRA (European Partnership on AI, Data and Robotics) and the GenAI4EU central Hub (CSA HORIZON-CL4-2025-03-HUMAN-18).

All research outputs — frameworks, models, benchmarks, the External Tool and API Orchestration Layer, and demonstrator results — will be shared with the European R&D community through the AI-on-demand platform; demonstrator results will be validated in Testing and Experiment Facilities (TEFs) and disseminated via European Digital Innovation Hubs (EDIHs).
MAESTRO links to resources offered by AI Factories and Data Labs for compute-intensive training and evaluation.

MAESTRO proposes demonstrator validation in operational environments achieving TRL 5 across two call-identified Apply AI sector demonstrators plus a cross-sector transfer demonstrator by the end of the project.

MAESTRO includes a Logistics and Supply Chain cross-sector transfer demonstrator — an agent-based planning system coordinating logistics operations across multiple actors (warehouses, carriers, customs) — explicitly described as validating architecture generalisability 'beyond the call's specifically identified Apply AI sectors' (healthcare, advanced manufacturing, in-vehicle autonomous driving).
scope_findings.flag_reason: Logistics and supply chain is not one of the three specifically identified Apply AI sectors in SR-07 (healthcare, advanced manufacturing, in-vehicle autonomous driving) and is not referenced by any Tier 2B scope element as a permitted or required demonstrator sector. The concept note itself explicitly acknowledges this is 'beyond the call's specifically identified Apply AI sectors.' MAESTRO satisfies SR-07 through its healthcare and advanced manufacturing demonstrators; the logistics demonstrator is framed as supplementary. No Tier 2B scope element explicitly excludes additional non-named sectors, but none confirms their scope-inclusion either. The scope boundary is defined by SR-07 which names three specific sectors, and CC-10 repeats those same three sectors in its portfolio coverage logic. The adequacy of this supplementary demonstrator to support or detract from the proposal's overall scope alignment cannot be confirmed from Tier 2B sources alone.

scope_findings.claim: MAESTRO includes a targeted architectural mapping analysis (WP8) assessing MAESTRO architecture transferability to in-vehicle autonomous driving settings, explicitly positioned as a bonus analysis rather than a full sector demonstrator, with the project acknowledging that the consortium lacks automotive sector partners.
scope_findings.flag_reason: In-vehicle autonomous driving is one of the three specifically identified Apply AI sectors in SR-07 and is named in CC-10's portfolio coverage constraint (which ensures at least one funded proposal focuses on each of the three sectors). The concept note and strategic positioning explicitly state MAESTRO will not deliver a demonstrator in in-vehicle autonomous driving due to the absence of automotive sector partners, substituting a WP8 architectural mapping analysis for in-vehicle autonomous driving transferability instead. SR-07 requires proposals to 'demonstrate application' in at least one sector (satisfied by healthcare and advanced manufacturing). However, CC-10's portfolio coverage logic creates evaluator-selection pressure for proposals with meaningful in-vehicle autonomous driving engagement. The scope boundary defined by SR-07 and CC-10 is ambiguous on whether a mapping analysis satisfies 'demonstrating application' in the sector, and no Tier 2B scope element confirms that a mapping analysis is an acceptable substitute. The claim is flagged rather than out-of-scope because no Tier 2B exclusion prohibits a mapping analysis and the activity is architecturally consistent with SR-01; however, the adequacy of this approach relative to the sector's portfolio-coverage status is unresolved and warrants explicit clarification in the proposal.

scope_findings.claim: MAESTRO's open-source framework and standardised agent interaction protocols will lower barriers for European SMEs and startups to build on next-generation AI agent technology, strengthening the European AI ecosystem's competitiveness and reducing strategic dependency on non-European AI platforms.
scope_findings.flag_reason: No Tier 2B scope element explicitly addresses open-source access for European SMEs and startups or strategic-dependency reduction framing as a scope requirement. The closest element is SR-10 and CC-05 (communicable results shared via the AI-on-demand platform), but those requirements do not mandate open-sourcing frameworks for SME accessibility or frame output dissemination in terms of reducing strategic dependency on non-European AI platforms. The claim is consistent with the broader Apply AI Strategy orientation of the call but cannot be confirmed in-scope from a specific Tier 2B scope element; scope boundary is defined by Tier 2B only per CLAUDE.md §13.2. The claim as framed introduces an impact/strategic-positioning assertion that goes beyond any SR or CC entry in the declared Tier 2B extracted files.
